title: Sticherus gleichenioides
intro: Sticherus gleichenioides, also known as the Australian Black Oak Fern, is a species of fern that belongs to the family Gleicheniaceae. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine, particularly in Australian Aboriginal cultures, to treat various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and skin irritations, due to its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Sticherus gleichenioides include triterpenoids, flavonoids, and saponins, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. Traditionally, the plant is used to make herbal teas, infusions, and poultices, and is often dried and used in tinctures and capsules for its medicinal benefits.
taxonomy: Sticherus gleichenioides is classified as a species of fern in the family Gleicheniaceae. The Gleicheniaceae family is a group of ferns commonly known as the chain ferns or wood ferns. This family is part of the more extensive order Gleicheniales. The order Gleicheniales is a group of ancient, primitive ferns that are thought to have evolved during the Mesozoic Era. The plant kingdom classification of Sticherus gleichenioides includes Kingdom: Plantae, Division: Pteridophyta, and Class: Polypodiopsida.
uses: Sticherus gleichenioides has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes, although its efficacy and safety may vary.
